title: Acute Traumatic CERVICAL Spinal Cord Injury (C5-C6) with NEUROGENIC SHOCK in a 24-Year-Old Man After a Shallow-Water Diving Accident: Distinguishing NEUROGENIC Shock (Hypotension + BRADYCARDIA + Warm Skin) from HYPOVOLEMIC Shock, Recognizing Impending NEUROMUSCULAR Respiratory Failure (C3-4-5 Keep the Diaphragm Alive), Maintaining a MAP Goal to Perfuse the Cord, Full SPINAL IMMOBILIZATION and Secondary-Injury Prevention, and Why Routine High-Dose METHYLPREDNISOLONE Is NO Longer Standard
subtitle: Acute traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) is a time-critical event in which the FIRST priority is preventing SECONDARY injury to a cord that has already been primarily damaged. This case centers on a high cervical (C5-C6) SCI from an axial-load (shallow-water diving) mechanism and the two phenomena that confuse learners: SPINAL SHOCK and NEUROGENIC SHOCK — which are NOT the same thing. SPINAL SHOCK is a NEUROLOGIC phenomenon: the transient loss of ALL reflexes, flaccid paralysis, and sensory loss BELOW the level of injury; it can last days to weeks, and the return of reflexes (classically the bulbocavernosus reflex) signals its resolution — it does NOT by itself predict permanent motor outcome. NEUROGENIC SHOCK is a HEMODYNAMIC (distributive) phenomenon seen with injuries at roughly T6 and ABOVE: loss of sympathetic outflow below the lesion produces unopposed PARASYMPATHETIC (vagal) tone, giving the classic TRIAD of HYPOTENSION + BRADYCARDIA + WARM, DRY, FLUSHED skin (peripheral vasodilation). This is the OPPOSITE of HYPOVOLEMIC/HEMORRHAGIC shock (tachycardia, cool/clammy/pale skin, narrow pulse pressure) — a key NCLEX discrimination. BUT a major-trauma patient can have BOTH, so the team must STILL complete the primary/secondary survey and exclude concurrent HEMORRHAGE (a normal hemoglobin and a normal lactate help argue against significant blood loss) before attributing all hypotension to neurogenic shock. AIRWAY/BREATHING is the highest acute priority: 'C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive' — the phrenic nerve arises from C3-C5, and lesions at or near C5 impair the intercostal and accessory muscles, leaving the patient dependent on diaphragmatic breathing with a weak cough and poor secretion clearance. Cord EDEMA can ASCEND one or two levels in the first 24-72 hours, threatening the diaphragm, so the nurse must trend SERIAL VITAL CAPACITY (VC), negative inspiratory force (NIF), respiratory rate/effort, ABGs, and secretions and anticipate ELECTIVE, CONTROLLED intubation (with MANUAL IN-LINE STABILIZATION, NOT neck extension) when mechanics decline — do NOT wait for a crash. CIRCULATION/PERFUSION: neurogenic shock is NOT primarily a volume problem, so the answer is NOT unlimited rapid fluid boluses (over-resuscitation -> pulmonary edema in a patient who cannot clear secretions); give CAUTIOUS isotonic fluid to a reasonable target and add VASOPRESSORS (norepinephrine provides alpha vasoconstriction PLUS beta chronotropy that helps the bradycardia; phenylephrine is pure alpha and can worsen reflex bradycardia). Treat symptomatic BRADYCARDIA with ATROPINE (transcutaneous pacing for refractory cases), and PRE-OXYGENATE before suctioning because vagal stimulation can trigger profound bradycardia/asystole. A higher MEAN ARTERIAL PRESSURE goal (commonly MAP >= 85-90 mmHg for ~7 days) is used to PERFUSE the injured cord and limit secondary ischemic injury. SPINAL IMMOBILIZATION/SECONDARY-INJURY PREVENTION: maintain a rigid cervical collar and full spine precautions, LOG-ROLL as a team keeping the spine in neutral ALIGNMENT (no flexion/rotation), obtain CT (and MRI for cord/ligament detail), and coordinate transfer to a spine/neurosurgical service. HIGH-DOSE METHYLPREDNISOLONE (the old NASCIS protocol) is CONTROVERSIAL and is NO LONGER routinely recommended — its uncertain benefit is outweighed by harms (infection, hyperglycemia, GI bleeding) — a classic 'what is NOT standard' test point. AUTONOMIC complications: POIKILOTHERMIA (the patient cannot shiver or sweat below the lesion -> regulate ambient temperature), NEUROGENIC BLADDER (indwelling catheter acutely), paralytic ILEUS (NPO + NG decompression), high VTE risk (mechanical +/- pharmacologic prophylaxis), pressure-injury risk (scheduled repositioning within precautions), and stress-ulcer risk (PPI/H2 prophylaxis). Later, once spinal shock resolves and reflexes return, the chronic complication of AUTONOMIC DYSREFLEXIA can emerge with lesions at/above T6 ([[case-873]]). CARE COORDINATION runs throughout: trauma activation, neurosurgery, respiratory therapy, critical care, PT/OT, social work, and — given the catastrophic, potentially life-altering nature of the injury in a young patient — early, honest, family-centered communication and psychosocial support. This case is DISTINCT from chronic SCI with autonomic dysreflexia ([[case-873]]) and from traumatic brain injury with increased ICP ([[case-1030]]). Step-6 evaluates whether each finding/response reflects IMPROVEMENT, WORSENING, or is UNCHANGED.

patient.name: T. Reyes
patient.admitDate: Today, 1432 — trauma activation; arrived by EMS on a backboard with a rigid cervical collar after diving headfirst into the shallow end of a pool (axial-load mechanism). Found by bystanders unable to move his legs and with weak arms. EMS reports persistent HYPOTENSION with a paradoxically SLOW heart rate and warm, dry skin despite the trauma. Primary survey: airway patent but breathing is shallow and diaphragmatic with a weak cough; quadriparesis with a sensory level at approximately C6, flaccid tone, and absent reflexes below the lesion. CT cervical spine shows a C5-C6 fracture-dislocation with cord compression. Admitted to the ICU on the trauma/neurosurgery service for neurogenic shock and impending neuromuscular respiratory failure.
patient.room: Trauma Bay 2 -> Neuro-Trauma ICU, Bed 7; mother en route; neurosurgery, respiratory therapy, and critical care at bedside

vitals.spo2: 93% on 4 L NC; skin WARM, DRY, and flushed below the clavicles; quadriparesis with sensory level ~C6, flaccid tone, areflexia below lesion; weak cough; temp 35.8°C; awake/oriented and frightened

94% on 4 L NC; cautious isotonic fluid given and NOREPINEPHRINE started and titrated to a MAP goal >= 85 mmHg; atropine at bedside for symptomatic bradycardia; trauma survey completed — no source of hemorrhage, hemoglobin and lactate normal

92% on 6 L; serial bedside VITAL CAPACITY declining (1.0 L) with a weak cough and rising PaCO2; elective, controlled intubation performed with MANUAL IN-LINE STABILIZATION before respiratory failure; full spine precautions maintained

98% intubated/ventilated; MAP goal >= 85 sustained for cord perfusion; indwelling urinary catheter (neurogenic bladder) and NG tube (paralytic ileus) placed; VTE and stress-ulcer prophylaxis started; warming measures for temp 35.9°C (poikilothermia)

98%; warm extremities with improving perfusion; sensory level stable at C6 (no ascent); scheduled q2h repositioning within spine precautions; neurosurgery planning surgical decompression/stabilization; mother at bedside and updated

98%; MAP maintained off pressors; bulbocavernosus reflex has returned (spinal shock resolving); PT/OT, SCI rehabilitation, and social work engaged; honest family-centered prognosis discussion with the patient and mother

labs.n: PRESENTATION + NEUROGENIC vs HYPOVOLEMIC shock (the core discrimination)
labs.v: 24 y/o man with a C5-C6 fracture-dislocation from an axial-load diving injury presents with HYPOTENSION (82/50), BRADYCARDIA (HR 46), and WARM/DRY/FLUSHED skin below the lesion — the classic triad of NEUROGENIC SHOCK. Neurogenic shock occurs with injuries at roughly T6 and ABOVE: loss of sympathetic outflow below the lesion leaves UNOPPOSED VAGAL tone -> peripheral VASODILATION (hypotension, warm skin) + BRADYCARDIA. This is the OPPOSITE of HYPOVOLEMIC/HEMORRHAGIC shock, which causes TACHYCARDIA with COOL, CLAMMY, PALE skin and a narrow pulse pressure. CRITICAL caveat: a major-trauma patient can have BOTH, so the team must STILL complete the primary/secondary survey and exclude HEMORRHAGE before attributing all hypotension to neurogenic shock — here a NORMAL hemoglobin and a NORMAL lactate argue against significant blood loss. Distinct from chronic SCI/autonomic dysreflexia ([[case-873]]) and TBI/increased ICP ([[case-1030]]).
labs.r: Neurogenic shock (injury ~T6 and above) = HYPOTENSION + BRADYCARDIA + WARM/DRY skin from lost sympathetic tone/unopposed vagal tone. Hypovolemic shock = TACHYCARDIA + COOL/CLAMMY/PALE skin. A trauma patient can have BOTH -> still rule out HEMORRHAGE (normal Hgb/lactate help). RN role: recognize the neurogenic triad AND keep looking for occult bleeding.

labs.n: SPINAL SHOCK vs NEUROGENIC SHOCK + NEUROMUSCULAR respiratory failure (C3-4-5)
labs.v: Do NOT confuse the two 'shocks.' SPINAL SHOCK is a NEUROLOGIC state: transient FLACCID paralysis, sensory loss, and AREFLEXIA below the lesion that can last days to weeks; the RETURN of reflexes (classically the BULBOCAVERNOSUS reflex) marks its resolution and does NOT by itself predict permanent motor recovery. NEUROGENIC SHOCK is the HEMODYNAMIC (distributive) picture above. RESPIRATORY: 'C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive' — the phrenic nerve arises from C3-C5. A C5-C6 lesion impairs intercostal/accessory muscles, leaving DIAPHRAGMATIC breathing with a WEAK COUGH and poor secretion clearance; cord EDEMA can ASCEND one or two levels in the first 24-72 h and threaten the diaphragm. Trend SERIAL VITAL CAPACITY (VC), NIF, RR/effort, ABGs, and secretions; anticipate ELECTIVE, CONTROLLED intubation with MANUAL IN-LINE STABILIZATION (NOT neck extension) when mechanics decline — do not wait for respiratory arrest.
labs.r: SPINAL shock = transient flaccidity/areflexia below lesion (neurologic; resolves when reflexes like bulbocavernosus return). NEUROGENIC shock = hemodynamic. RESP: C3-4-5 = diaphragm; high cervical -> diaphragmatic breathing + weak cough + ascending-edema risk -> trend VC/NIF/ABG and intubate ELECTIVELY with IN-LINE stabilization before failure. RN role: serial respiratory mechanics + early controlled airway.

labs.n: Hemodynamic management (MAP goal, cautious fluids, vasopressors, atropine) + SPINAL IMMOBILIZATION + methylprednisolone controversy
labs.v: Neurogenic shock is NOT primarily a volume problem, so the answer is NOT unlimited rapid fluid boluses (over-resuscitation -> PULMONARY EDEMA in a patient who cannot clear secretions). Give CAUTIOUS isotonic fluid to a reasonable target, then add VASOPRESSORS: NOREPINEPHRINE is preferred (alpha vasoconstriction PLUS beta chronotropy that supports the slow heart rate); PHENYLEPHRINE is pure alpha and can WORSEN reflex bradycardia. Treat symptomatic BRADYCARDIA with ATROPINE (transcutaneous pacing if refractory), and PRE-OXYGENATE before suctioning because vagal stimulation can trigger profound bradycardia/asystole. Use a higher MEAN ARTERIAL PRESSURE goal (commonly MAP >= 85-90 mmHg for ~7 days) to PERFUSE the injured cord and limit secondary ischemia. IMMOBILIZATION: keep the rigid cervical collar and full spine precautions, LOG-ROLL as a team in NEUTRAL ALIGNMENT (no flexion/rotation), obtain CT (and MRI for cord), and coordinate neurosurgical decompression/stabilization. HIGH-DOSE METHYLPREDNISOLONE (old NASCIS protocol) is CONTROVERSIAL and NO LONGER routinely recommended — uncertain benefit, real harms (infection, hyperglycemia, GI bleeding).
labs.r: Neurogenic shock: CAUTIOUS isotonic fluids (not unlimited boluses -> pulmonary edema) + VASOPRESSOR (NOREPINEPHRINE preferred; phenylephrine can worsen bradycardia) + ATROPINE/pacing for symptomatic bradycardia; PRE-OXYGENATE before suction. MAP goal ~>= 85-90 mmHg x ~7 days to perfuse the cord. Keep collar + spine precautions + LOG-ROLL in alignment; CT/MRI; neurosurgery. Routine high-dose methylprednisolone = NOT standard. RN role: titrate pressor to MAP, treat bradycardia, protect alignment.

labs.v: Autonomic disruption below the lesion produces: POIKILOTHERMIA (cannot shiver or sweat -> regulate AMBIENT temperature, use warming/cooling); NEUROGENIC BLADDER (indwelling catheter acutely; later intermittent catheterization); paralytic ILEUS/gastric atony (NPO + NG decompression, then advance); high VTE risk (mechanical +/- pharmacologic prophylaxis once bleeding risk allows); PRESSURE-INJURY risk (scheduled q2h repositioning WITHIN spine precautions, padding, heels off bed); and stress-ulcer risk (PPI/H2 prophylaxis). Once spinal shock resolves and reflexes return, the CHRONIC complication of AUTONOMIC DYSREFLEXIA can emerge with lesions at/above T6 ([[case-873]]). CARE COORDINATION: trauma activation, neurosurgery, RT, critical care, PT/OT, SCI rehab, social work, and EARLY, honest, family-centered communication given the life-altering injury in a young patient. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT flood him with rapid boluses as sole therapy; did NOT use phenylephrine-only/ignore the bradycardia; did NOT delay airway support until arrest; did NOT extend the neck or remove precautions before clearance; did NOT give routine high-dose methylprednisolone; did NOT forget VTE/skin/bladder/bowel/temperature/stress-ulcer care.
labs.r: Complications: poikilothermia (regulate ambient temp), neurogenic bladder (catheter), ileus (NPO/NG), VTE (prophylaxis), pressure injury (q2h within precautions), stress ulcer (PPI/H2); later AUTONOMIC DYSREFLEXIA at/above T6 ([[case-873]]). Coordinate trauma/neurosurg/RT/PT-OT/rehab/social work + honest family communication. Negatives confirm correct practice (no fluid-only resuscitation, treat bradycardia, early controlled airway, keep precautions, no routine steroids, do all immobility prophylaxis).

labs.v: Sinus BRADYCARDIA 46 bpm with hypotension and warm skin (neurogenic shock; unopposed vagal tone)
1.0 L and DECLINING (~13 mL/kg) with a weak cough — approaching the threshold for intubation
1.7 mmol/L (NORMAL — argues against hemorrhagic/hypovolemic shock and tissue hypoperfusion from blood loss)
13.8 g/dL (NORMAL — no evidence of significant hemorrhage; supports a neurogenic rather than hypovolemic cause of hypotension)

nursesNote: 1435 — Trauma activation. 24 y/o man, headfirst dive into shallow water (axial load), arrived on backboard with rigid C-collar. Primary survey: airway patent; breathing SHALLOW/DIAPHRAGMATIC with weak cough, SpO2 93% on 4 L. Circulation: BP 82/50 with HR 46 (BRADYCARDIA) and WARM, DRY, FLUSHED skin below the clavicles = NEUROGENIC SHOCK picture. Neuro: quadriparesis, sensory level ~C6, flaccid tone, areflexia below lesion; awake/oriented/frightened. CT C-spine: C5-C6 fracture-dislocation with cord compression. Trauma secondary survey completed — NO source of hemorrhage; Hgb and lactate normal (argues against hypovolemic shock). Hemodynamics: cautious isotonic fluid + NOREPINEPHRINE titrated to MAP >= 85 mmHg for cord perfusion; atropine at bedside for symptomatic bradycardia; pre-oxygenate before any suctioning (vagal bradycardia risk). Respiratory: serial VITAL CAPACITY trended — declining to 1.0 L with rising PaCO2 -> ELECTIVE, CONTROLLED intubation with MANUAL IN-LINE STABILIZATION before respiratory failure; full spine precautions maintained throughout. Immobilization: C-collar + spine precautions, team LOG-ROLL in neutral alignment, no neck extension; MRI ordered; neurosurgery for decompression/stabilization. Did NOT give routine high-dose methylprednisolone (not standard). Complications addressed: indwelling urinary catheter (neurogenic bladder), NG tube + NPO (paralytic ileus), VTE prophylaxis, q2h repositioning within precautions + skin/heel care, stress-ulcer prophylaxis, and WARMING measures (poikilothermia, temp 35.8). Monitoring for ascending sensory level/respiratory decline, vagal bradycardia with suctioning, and later autonomic dysreflexia. Mother en route; honest, family-centered communication and SCI rehab/social work engaged.
providerOrders: TRAUMA/ICU ADMIT: C5-C6 fracture-dislocation with cord compression and neurogenic shock; maintain FULL SPINAL PRECAUTIONS and rigid cervical collar until cleared/stabilized; continuous cardiac, BP (arterial line), and SpO2 monitoring with frequent neuro checks

AIRWAY/BREATHING: oxygen to keep SpO2 >= 94%; trend BEDSIDE VITAL CAPACITY/NIF, respiratory rate/effort, and ABGs; ELECTIVE intubation with MANUAL IN-LINE STABILIZATION for declining mechanics/rising PaCO2; PRE-OXYGENATE before suctioning (vagal bradycardia risk); pulmonary toilet

HEMODYNAMICS: cautious isotonic crystalloid to a reasonable target (avoid over-resuscitation/pulmonary edema); NOREPINEPHRINE titrated to MAP >= 85-90 mmHg for ~7 days to perfuse the cord; ATROPINE 0.5 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia, transcutaneous pacing if refractory
EXCLUDE HEMORRHAGE: complete trauma primary/secondary survey; type and screen, CBC, lactate; FAST/CT as indicated before attributing all hypotension to neurogenic shock
IMAGING/SURGERY: CT cervical spine; MRI for cord/ligamentous injury; neurosurgery consult for decompression and stabilization; LOG-ROLL with adequate staff maintaining neutral alignment
DO NOT give routine high-dose IV methylprednisolone (NASCIS protocol) — not standard of care; uncertain benefit with infection/hyperglycemia/GI-bleed harms
NEUROGENIC BLADDER/BOWEL: insert indwelling urinary catheter; NPO with NASOGASTRIC tube for paralytic ileus; bowel regimen once ileus resolves

PROPHYLAXIS/SKIN: VTE prophylaxis (mechanical now, add pharmacologic when bleeding risk permits); stress-ulcer prophylaxis (PPI/H2); scheduled q2h repositioning WITHIN spine precautions, pressure-injury prevention, heels off bed
TEMPERATURE: monitor for POIKILOTHERMIA; regulate ambient temperature and use warming/cooling measures as needed
CARE COORDINATION: PT/OT, SCI rehabilitation, respiratory therapy, social work, and case management; provide early, honest, family-centered communication and psychosocial support

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this trauma patient?

opts.text: Hypotension (82/50) WITH bradycardia (HR 46) and warm, dry, flushed skin below the lesion — the classic neurogenic-shock triad
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — in a high cervical SCI this triad (hypotension + bradycardia + warm skin) is neurogenic shock from lost sympathetic tone; it is the OPPOSITE of hypovolemic shock.

opts.text: Quadriparesis with a sensory level at ~C6, flaccid tone, and absent reflexes below the lesion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — acute SCI with spinal shock (flaccidity/areflexia); the level near C5-C6 also threatens respiratory muscles.

opts.text: Shallow, diaphragmatic respirations with a weak cough and a declining bedside vital capacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — impending NEUROMUSCULAR respiratory failure (C3-4-5 keep the diaphragm alive); demands serial VC/NIF/ABG and anticipation of elective intubation.

opts.text: Axial-load (shallow-water diving) mechanism with a rigid collar in place
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-energy axial loading risks an unstable cervical fracture; maintain full spinal immobilization to prevent secondary cord injury.

opts.text: Despite the neurogenic picture, the trauma survey must still exclude concurrent hemorrhage (normal hemoglobin and lactate here)
opts.rat: RELEVANT — a trauma patient can have BOTH neurogenic and hypovolemic shock; normal Hgb/lactate help argue against significant blood loss, but the survey is mandatory.

opts.text: Temperature 35.8°C with an inability to shiver
opts.rat: RELEVANT — poikilothermia from autonomic disruption below the lesion; the patient cannot regulate temperature and needs ambient warming.

opts.text: The warm skin and normal lactate prove perfusion is adequate, so the slow heart rate and low BP need no monitoring or treatment
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Warm skin reflects vasodilation, not adequacy; symptomatic bradycardia and hypotension in neurogenic shock require active treatment (pressors, atropine) and continuous monitoring.

opts.text: Because he is awake and talking, the airway is secure and respiratory status needs no further attention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A high cervical SCI can fatigue and decompensate over hours as cord edema ascends; respiratory mechanics must be trended and the airway secured electively before failure.

opts.text: Neurogenic shock results from loss of sympathetic outflow below the lesion -> vasodilation (hypotension, warm skin) plus unopposed vagal tone (bradycardia); it differs from hypovolemic shock, which causes tachycardia with cool, clammy skin
opts.rat: CORRECT — the defining contrast and the reason management differs (vasopressors/atropine vs. volume).

opts.text: Spinal shock is a transient NEUROLOGIC loss of reflexes/flaccidity below the lesion; its resolution (e.g., return of the bulbocavernosus reflex) is expected and does not by itself predict permanent motor outcome
opts.rat: CORRECT — spinal shock is distinct from the hemodynamic neurogenic shock and its resolution is a neurologic milestone, not a prognosis.

opts.text: A lesion near C5 impairs intercostal/accessory muscles and leaves the patient diaphragm-dependent; cord edema may ascend a level or two, so respiratory mechanics can worsen even if the patient looks stable now
opts.rat: CORRECT — the rationale for serial VC/NIF/ABG monitoring and elective, controlled intubation.

opts.text: Maintaining a higher MAP goal (commonly >= 85-90 mmHg) supports spinal cord perfusion and may limit secondary ischemic injury
opts.rat: CORRECT — perfusion of the injured cord is a guideline-based hemodynamic target in acute SCI.

opts.text: Autonomic disruption explains the poikilothermia, neurogenic bladder, and paralytic ileus — all expected early problems requiring proactive management
opts.rat: CORRECT — these are predictable autonomic/immobility complications to anticipate and prevent.

opts.text: The priority drug is high-dose IV methylprednisolone, which is the proven standard that restores neurologic function
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine high-dose methylprednisolone is controversial and NO longer recommended; benefit is unproven and harms (infection, hyperglycemia, GI bleeding) are real.

opts.text: Because the skin looks warm and well-perfused, vasopressors are contraindicated and the hypotension should be ignored
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The warmth IS the vasodilation causing hypotension; vasopressors (norepinephrine) are a mainstay to restore vascular tone and MAP.

opts.text: Protect AIRWAY/BREATHING — trend respiratory mechanics and intubate electively (with in-line stabilization) before neuromuscular respiratory failure
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. A high cervical lesion can fatigue the diaphragm/ascend; airway/ventilation is the most immediately life-threatening axis.

opts.text: Restore CIRCULATION/perfusion — cautious fluids plus vasopressors to a MAP goal and atropine for symptomatic bradycardia, while excluding hemorrhage
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Sustained hypotension/bradycardia threatens cord and organ perfusion; treat the neurogenic shock and rule out concurrent bleeding.

opts.text: Prevent SECONDARY cord injury — maintain spinal immobilization/neutral alignment, log-roll, image, and coordinate neurosurgical stabilization
opts.rat: #3 — Protecting the already-injured cord is critical and runs concurrently, but airway and perfusion are the immediate survival priorities.

opts.text: Prevent immobility/autonomic complications — VTE, pressure injury, neurogenic bladder/ileus, stress ulcer, and poikilothermia
opts.rat: #4 — Important proactive prevention that begins early but is secondary to the acute ABC threats and cord protection.

opts.text: Coordinate rehabilitation and provide psychosocial/family support for the life-altering injury
opts.rat: #5 — Essential to long-term outcome and humane care, but lower acuity than the immediate physiologic threats.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Maintain the rigid cervical collar and full spine precautions; log-roll as a team keeping the spine in neutral alignment
opts.rat: Prevents secondary cord injury until the spine is stabilized/cleared.

opts.text: Trend serial vital capacity/NIF/ABGs and prepare for elective intubation with manual in-line stabilization if mechanics decline
opts.rat: Anticipates neuromuscular respiratory failure and secures the airway before a crash, protecting the cervical spine during intubation.

opts.text: Give cautious isotonic fluids and titrate norepinephrine to a MAP goal (>= 85-90 mmHg); give atropine for symptomatic bradycardia
opts.rat: Restores vascular tone and perfuses the injured cord; norepinephrine also supports the heart rate, and atropine treats vagal bradycardia.

opts.text: Complete the trauma survey and check hemoglobin/lactate to exclude concurrent hemorrhage
opts.rat: A trauma patient can have both neurogenic and hypovolemic shock; occult bleeding must be excluded.

opts.text: Insert a urinary catheter and NG tube, start VTE and stress-ulcer prophylaxis, reposition q2h within precautions, and regulate ambient temperature
opts.rat: Proactively manages neurogenic bladder, ileus, VTE/pressure-injury/ulcer risk, and poikilothermia.

opts.text: Pre-oxygenate before tracheal suctioning and keep atropine available
opts.rat: Vagal stimulation during suctioning can trigger profound bradycardia/asystole in SCI; pre-oxygenation and ready atropine mitigate this.

opts.text: Treat the hypotension and bradycardia with rapid, repeated large fluid boluses as the sole therapy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — neurogenic shock is not primarily volume loss; over-resuscitation causes pulmonary edema and does not correct the vasodilation/bradycardia. Use cautious fluids PLUS vasopressors/atropine.

opts.text: Administer routine high-dose IV methylprednisolone per the NASCIS protocol
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — routine high-dose steroids are no longer standard; unproven benefit with significant harms.

opts.text: Remove the collar and perform active neck range-of-motion to assess his mobility
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — moving an unstable cervical spine risks catastrophic secondary cord injury; precautions stay until cleared/stabilized.

opts.text: Use phenylephrine alone and accept the bradycardia as harmless
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — pure alpha agents can worsen reflex bradycardia; norepinephrine is preferred, and symptomatic bradycardia must be treated.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess and support airway/breathing — apply oxygen, trend vital capacity/SpO2/ABG, and ready elective intubation with in-line stabilization
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Airway and ventilation are the most immediately life-threatening axis in a high cervical SCI.

opts.text: Establish IV access, give cautious isotonic fluid, and titrate norepinephrine to the MAP goal with atropine ready for symptomatic bradycardia
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Restores perfusion to the cord and organs and treats the neurogenic-shock hemodynamics.

opts.text: Maintain spinal immobilization, log-roll for assessment, and facilitate CT/MRI and neurosurgical evaluation
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Protects the cord from secondary injury and advances definitive stabilization.

opts.text: Place the urinary catheter and NG tube; start VTE/stress-ulcer prophylaxis, q2h repositioning, and temperature regulation
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Proactive prevention of autonomic and immobility complications.

opts.text: Provide honest, family-centered communication and engage SCI rehabilitation and social work
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Supports the patient/family and sets up long-term recovery once the patient is stabilized.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each finding as the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or UNCHANGED.

opts.text: On titrated norepinephrine the MAP is sustained >= 85 mmHg, the heart rate is now in the 60s, and the extremities are warm with good urine output
opts.rat: Hemodynamic goals met — adequate cord/organ perfusion with resolution of symptomatic bradycardia.

opts.text: After elective intubation the ABG normalizes and the ventilated patient is comfortable with stable mechanics
opts.rat: Controlled airway management corrected the neuromuscular hypoventilation before a crisis.

opts.text: On Day 4 the bulbocavernosus reflex returns
opts.rat: Resolution of spinal shock — an expected neurologic milestone (note: it marks the end of spinal shock, not a guarantee of motor recovery).

opts.text: The sensory level ascends from C6 to C4 with a falling vital capacity and a rising PaCO2
opts.rat: ⚠️ Ascending cord edema with impending respiratory failure — escalate immediately and secure the airway.

opts.text: During tracheal suctioning the heart rate drops to 38 with hypotension
opts.rat: ⚠️ Vagally mediated bradycardia — stop suctioning, oxygenate, and give atropine; pre-oxygenate before future suctioning.

opts.text: He develops a fever, foul-smelling urine, and a rising WBC with the indwelling catheter in place
opts.rat: ⚠️ Catheter-associated UTI — an immobility/device complication requiring treatment and catheter reassessment.

opts.text: A new warm, swollen, tender calf is noted on Day 3
opts.rat: ⚠️ Suspected DVT — high VTE risk in SCI; evaluate and ensure prophylaxis is optimized.

opts.text: Flaccid paralysis and areflexia below the lesion persist in the first hours after injury
opts.rat: Consistent with ongoing spinal shock in the acute phase — expected and too early to prognosticate.

opts.text: Off vasopressors on Day 4, the MAP stays >= 85 mmHg with warm, well-perfused skin
opts.rat: Resolving neurogenic shock — vascular tone recovering enough to maintain perfusion without pharmacologic support.
